questions.question: Why is a single consistency answer usually weak for a global system?
options: Different workflows have different freshness and correctness needs, so one mode rarely fits all of them well.
Because consistency only matters for batch systems.
Because global systems never replicate data.
Because eventual consistency is always enough.
questions.explanation: Senior answers match semantics to workflow rather than using one blanket label.

questions.question: What does long inter-region RTT usually do to strongly coordinated writes?
options: It raises write latency and can reduce availability during partitions.
It makes writes cheaper than local writes.
It removes the need for repair tooling.
It guarantees read-your-writes everywhere.
questions.explanation: Distance makes stronger coordination slower and more failure-sensitive.

questions.question: When is a home-region write model especially attractive?
options: When per-entity ownership is clear and you want simpler correctness than global multi-writer coordination.
When every request must be served from every region equally.
When repair queues are desirable for their own sake.
When product requirements forbid naming ownership.
questions.explanation: Home-region ownership contains conflicts and simplifies write semantics.

questions.question: What is the strongest reason to define bounded staleness explicitly?
options: It makes the freshness trade-off measurable and easier to align with product expectations.
It guarantees strong consistency.
It removes the need for replication lag metrics.
It only matters for analytics systems.
questions.explanation: Explicit bounds turn a vague trade-off into an observable promise.

questions.question: Which signal best shows that multi-writer design is becoming operationally unsafe?
options: Conflict volume and repair backlog are growing faster than the system can reconcile them.
Static asset cache hit ratio is improving.
A single dashboard loads slowly in the browser.
The company opened a new office.
questions.explanation: Conflict and repair load reveal whether the chosen write model is sustainable.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with routing critical reads to a home region?
options: Stronger freshness at the cost of higher latency for those reads.
Lower latency and lower cost with no downside.
Automatic elimination of partitions.
Removal of the need for local replicas.

questions.question: Why should partition behavior be defined per workflow?
options: Some operations should fail closed, some can go read-only, and some can tolerate stale reads.
All partitions have identical product impact.
Partitions only affect control planes.
Undefined behavior gives operators more flexibility.
questions.explanation: Not every workflow should degrade the same way.

questions.question: What is the clearest sign of a strong interview answer on geo-consistency?
options: It ties semantics to concrete user workflows, latency cost, failure behavior, and repair strategy.
It only names CAP theorem terms.
It avoids numbers and RTT discussion.
It assumes global strong consistency is free if the company is large enough.
questions.explanation: High-signal answers are concrete about both cost and user-visible behavior.
